5.o: The ark was a type of Christ, and, as such, a token of the presence of God. That gracious promise, Lo, I am with you alway, even unto the end of the world, does, in effect, bring the ark into our religious assemblies, if we by faith and prayer plead that promise; and this we should be most earnest for. When Christ is formed in a soul, the law written in the heart, the ark of the covenant settled there, so that it becomes the temple of the Holy Ghost, there is true satisfaction in that soul.
5.m: The ark was like Jesus, showing where God was. The promise "I will always be with you until the end of the world" brings the ark into our church meetings if we ask for it by faith and prayer. We should really want this to happen. When Jesus forms a person’s soul, writes his laws on their heart, and settles the covenant there, making them a home for the Holy Spirit, then that person has real peace inside.

5.o: God took possession of the temple; he filled it with a cloud. Thus he signified his acceptance of this temple, to be the same to him that the tabernacle of Moses was, and assured his people that he would be the same in it. Would we have God dwell in our hearts, we must leave room for him; every thing else must give way. The Word was made flesh; and when he comes to his temple, like a refiner's fire, who may abide the day of his coming? May he prepare us for that day.
5.m:
God showed he accepted the temple by filling it with a cloud. This meant he saw it as just as good as the old tabernacle and promised to be present there too. 

If we want God to live in our hearts, we need to make space for him. Everything else has to move out. When Jesus came to earth as a human, he came to his temple like a purifying fire. Who can stand on the day he returns?

May he help us get ready for that day.

9.o: This history has been considered, 1Ki 10; yet because our Saviour has proposed it as an example in seeking after him, Mt 12:42, we must not pass it over without observing, that those who know the worth of true wisdom will grudge no pains or cost to obtain it. The queen of Sheba put herself to a great deal of trouble and expense to hear the wisdom of Solomon; and yet, learning from him to serve God, and do her duty, she thought herself well paid for her pains. Heavenly wisdom is that pearl of great price, for which, if we part with all, we make a good bargain.
9.m:
This story has been discussed before, 1 Kings 10, but since Jesus used it as an example, Matthew 12:42, we should pay attention to it again. People who value real wisdom won't mind putting in effort or spending money to get it. 

The queen of Sheba went through a lot of trouble and spent a lot of money just to learn Solomon’s wisdom. After learning about serving God and doing what was right, she felt it was worth it. 

Heavenly wisdom is like a valuable pearl. If we give up everything else to have it, we make a good choice.

9.o: The imports here mentioned, would show that prosperity drew the minds of Solomon and his subjects to the love of things curious and uncommon, though useless in themselves. True wisdom and happiness are always united together; but no such alliance exists between wealth and the enjoyment of the things of this life. Let us then acquaint ourselves with the Saviour, that we may find rest for our souls. Here is Solomon reigning in wealth and power, in ease and fulness, the like of which could never since be found; for the most known of the great princes of the earth were famed for their wars; whereas Solomon reigned forty years in profound peace. The promise was fulfilled, that God would give him riches and honour, such as no kings have had or shall have. The lustre wherein he appeared, was typical of the spiritual glory of the kingdom of the Messiah, and but a faint representation of His throne, which is above every throne. Here is Solomon dying, and leaving all his wealth and power to one who he knew would be a fool! Ec 2:18, 19. This was not only vanity, but vexation of spirit. Neither power, wealth, nor wisdom, can ward off or prepare for the stroke of death. But thanks be to God who giveth the victory to the true believer, even over this dreaded enemy, through Jesus Christ our Lord.
9.m:
Here are the imports mentioned showing that prosperity made Solomon and his people interested in fancy and unusual things, even if those things weren't useful. True wisdom and happiness go hand in hand, but money doesn’t bring joy by itself. We should get to know the Savior so we can find peace for our souls.

Solomon ruled with great wealth and power, living comfortably and richly, more than anyone has ever done since. Most famous rulers were known for their battles, but Solomon ruled peacefully for forty years. God’s promise came true—He gave Solomon riches and honor greater than any other king before or after him. 

Solomon’s greatness pointed to the future glory of the Messiah’s kingdom, a pale comparison to the real thing. When Solomon died, he left behind all his wealth and power to someone he knew would be foolish. This showed that having lots of stuff didn’t matter because death takes everything away. Only faith in Jesus gives us victory over death.

11.o: A few good words might have prevented the rebellion of Rehoboam's subjects; but all the force of his kingdom cannot bring them back. And it is in vain to contend with the purpose of God, when it is made known to us. Even those who are destitute of true faith, will at times pay some regard to the word of God, and be kept by it from wrong actions, to which they are prone by nature.
11.m: A few kind words could have stopped Rehoboam’s people from rebelling. But now, even all the power of his kingdom cannot bring them back. It is useless to fight against God’s plan once we know about it. Sometimes, people without real faith still listen to what God says and avoid doing bad things they naturally want to do.

11.o: When the priests and Levites came to Jerusalem, the devout, pious Israelites followed them. Such as set their hearts to seek the Lord God of Israel, left the inheritance of their fathers, and went to Jerusalem, that they might have free access to the altar of God, and be out of the temptation to worship the calves. That is best for us, which is best for our souls; in all our choices, religious advantages must be sought before all outward conveniences. Where God's faithful priests are, his faithful people should be. And when it has been proved that we are willing to renounce our worldly interests, so far as we are called to do so for the sake of Christ and his gospel, we have good evidence that we are truly his disciples. And it is the interest of a nation to protect religion and religious people.
11.m:
When the priests and Levites went to Jerusalem, many devout Israelites followed them. Those who wanted to find the Lord God of Israel left their homes and traveled to Jerusalem. They did this so they could freely go to God’s altar without being tempted to worship idols.

What is best for us is what is best for our souls. In making decisions, we should look for spiritual benefits first, even if it means giving up some comforts. 

Where there are faithful priests, there should be faithful followers. When we show that we are ready to give up our worldly things for Jesus and his teachings, we prove that we really belong to him.

It’s good for a country to support its religious people and their beliefs.

20.o: In all dangers, public or personal, our first business should be to seek help from God. Hence the advantage of days for national fasting and prayer. From the first to the last of our seeking the Lord, we must approach him with humiliation for our sins, trusting only in his mercy and power. Jehoshaphat acknowledges the sovereign dominion of the Divine Providence. Lord, exert it on our behalf. Whom should we seek to, whom should we trust to for relief, but the God we have chosen and served. Those that use what they have for God, may comfortably hope he will secure it to them. Every true believer is a son of Abraham, a friend of God; with such the everlasting covenant is established, to such every promise belongs. We are assured of God's love, by his dwelling in human nature in the person of the Saviour. Jehoshaphat mentions the temple, as a token of God's favourable presence. He pleads the injustice of his enemies. We may well appeal to God against those that render us evil for good. Though he had a great army, he said, We have no might without thee; we rely upon thee.
20.m:
When facing any danger, whether big or small, our main task is to ask God for help. That’s why having special days for the whole country to fast and pray is helpful. 

From the start to the end of asking God for help, we need to show humility about our wrongdoings and put our full trust in his kindness and strength. 

Jehoshaphat recognizes that God controls everything through his divine providence. So he asks God to act on their behalf. Who else would we turn to for help except the God we serve? 

People who use what they have for God can hope that he will protect it for them. Anyone who truly believes in God is a child of Abraham and a friend of God. The eternal promises apply to these believers. 

We know God loves us because he came down to earth in the form of Jesus. 

Jehoshaphat talks about the temple as a sign that God is near and present. He points out how unfair his enemies are. It’s right to tell God about people who treat us badly instead of kindly. 

Even though Jehoshaphat had a large army, he still said, “Without you, we have no strength.” They trusted completely in God.

20.o: The Spirit of prophecy came upon a Levite in the midst of the congregation. The Spirit, like the wind, blows where and on whom He listeth. He encouraged them to trust in God. Let the Christian soldier go out against his spiritual enemies, and the God of peace will make him more than a conqueror. Our trials will prove our gain. The advantage will be all our own, but the whole glory must be given to God.
20.m:
A Levite in the middle of the group was filled with the Spirit of prophecy. Just like the wind, the Spirit moves wherever and whenever it wants. 

He told everyone to have faith in God. When a believer fights their spiritual battles, God will help them win.

Our struggles will actually turn out good for us. We get the benefits, but we should give all the credit to God.

20.o: Jehoshaphat exhorted his troops to firm faith in God. Faith inspires a man with true courage; nor will any thing help more to the establishing of the heart in shaking times, than a firm belief of the power, and mercy, and promise of God. In all our trust in the Lord, and our praises of him, let us especially look at his everlasting mercy to sinners through Jesus Christ. Never was an army so destroyed as that of the enemy. Thus God often makes wicked people destroy one another. And never was a victory celebrated with more solemn thanksgivings.
20.m:
Jehoshaphat encouraged his soldiers to have strong faith in God. Faith gives real courage. Nothing helps keep someone brave better than believing firmly in God’s power, mercy, and promises. When we trust in the Lord and praise him, we should focus on his endless mercy for sinners through Jesus Christ.

The enemy’s army was completely destroyed—never before had such a total defeat happened. Often, God lets evil people destroy each other. This victory was marked by very serious thanksgiving celebrations.

20.o: Jehoshaphat kept close to the worship of God, and did what he could to keep his people close to it. But after God had done such great things for him, given him not only victory, but wealth; after this, to go and join himself with a wicked king, was very ungrateful. What could he expect but that God would be angry with him? Yet it seems, he took the warning; for when Ahaziah afterward pressed him to join him, he would not, 1Ki 22:49. Thus the alliance was broken, and the Divine rebuke had its effect, at least for a season. Let us be thankful for any losses which may have prevented the loss of our immortal souls. Let us praise the Lord, who sought after us, and left us not to perish in our sins.
20.m:
Jehoshaphat stayed faithful to worshipping God and tried his best to keep his people doing the same. But after God helped him so much—giving him victories and riches—it was really unkind of Jehoshaphat to then team up with an evil king. How else could God react except by being upset?

But it looks like Jehoshaphat learned from this. Later, when another bad king named Ahaziah asked him to join forces again, Jehoshaphat said no, as we see in 1 Kings 22:49. This shows that the warning worked—at least for a while.

We should be grateful if anything stops us from losing our eternal soul. We should thank the Lord for seeking us out and saving us from our sins.

21.o: Jehoram hated his brethren, and slew them, for the same reason that Cain hated Abel, and slew him, because their piety condemned his impiety. In the mystery of Providence such men sometimes prosper for a time; but the Lord has righteous purposes in permitting such events, part of which may now be made out, and the rest will be seen hereafter.
21.m: Jehoram hated his brothers and killed them, just like Cain hated Abel and killed him. Jehoram did this because he was jealous of their goodness compared to his own badness. Sometimes God lets people who do evil things succeed for a while. But God has reasons for allowing these things to happen, some we see now and others we will understand later.

21.o: A warning from God was sent to Jehoram. The Spirit of prophecy might direct Elijah to prepare this writing in the foresight of Jehoram's crimes. He is plainly told that his sin should certainly ruin him. But no marvel that sinners are not frightened from sin, and to repentance, by the threatenings of misery in another world, when the certainty of misery in this world, the sinking of their estates, and the ruin of their health, will not restrain them from vicious courses. See Jehoram here stripped of all his comforts. Thus God plainly showed that the controversy was with him, and his house. He had slain all his brethren to strengthen himself; now, all his sons are slain but one. David's house must not be wholly destroyed, like those of Israel's kings, because a blessing was in it; that of the Messiah. Good men may be afflicted with diseases; but to them they are fatherly chastisements, and by the support of Divine consolations the soul may dwell at ease, even when the body lies in pain. To be sick and poor, sick and solitary, but especially to be sick and in sin, sick and under the curse of God, sick and without grace to bear it, is a most deplorable case. Wickedness and profaneness make men despicable, even in the eyes of those who have but little religion.
21.m:
God gave Jehoram a warning through His prophet Elijah. Elijah wrote down the message before he knew about Jehoram’s future sins. 

Jehoram is clearly told that his actions would surely lead to his downfall. It’s not surprising that people don’t stop doing wrong things just because they’re threatened with punishment after death. Even knowing for sure that their money will run out and their health will fail doesn't keep some from being bad.

Here, Jehoram loses everything good in life. This shows that God is against him and his family. Jehoram killed all his brothers to gain power. Now, almost all his children die except one.

David’s family wasn’t completely destroyed because God promised to bless them with the Messiah. 

Good people can get sick too, but sickness is meant to correct them. With God’s help, their souls stay peaceful even if their bodies hurt.

Being sick and poor, sick and alone, or worst of all, sick and hated by God, is very sad. When someone is sinful, ungodly, and has no comfort from God, their situation is truly terrible.

People who act badly and disrespect God are looked down upon, even by those who aren’t very religious themselves.

24.o: Joash is more zealous about the repair of the temple than Jehoiada himself. It is easier to build temples, than to be temples to God. But the repairing of places for public worship is a good work, which all should promote. And many a good work would be done that now lies undone, if active men would put it forward.
24.m: Joash was even more eager to fix up the temple than Jehoiada was. Building places for worship is easier than becoming people who truly honor God. Still, fixing up places where people go to worship is a great thing, and everyone should help with it. Many good things could get done if more active people would step in and make them happen.

24.o: See what a great judgment on any prince or people, the death of godly, zealous, useful men is. See how necessary it is that we act in religion from inward principle. Then the loss of a parent, a minister, or a friend, will not be losing our religion. Often both princes and inferior people have been flattered to their ruin. True grace alone will enable a man to bring forth fruit unto the end. Zechariah, the son of Jehoiada, being filled with the Spirit of prophecy, stood up, and told the people of their sin. This is the work of ministers, by the word of God, as a lamp and a light, to discover the sin of men, and expound the providences of God. They stoned Zechariah to death in the court of the house of the Lord. Observe the dying martyr's words: The Lord look upon it, and require it! This came not from a spirit of revenge, but a spirit of prophecy. God smote Joash with great diseases, of body, or mind, or both, before the Syrians departed from him. If vengeance pursue men, the end of one trouble will be but the beginning of another. His own servants slew him. These judgments are called the burdens laid upon him, for the wrath of God is a heavy burden, too heavy for any man to bear. May God help us to take warning, to be upright in heart, and to persevere in his ways to the end.
24.m:
Here’s the passage rewritten in simpler, clearer language while keeping all the original ideas intact:

---

Notice how sad it is when good religious leaders die. It shows how important it is to follow religion out of true belief, not just because someone else tells you to. When a parent, teacher, or friend who teaches you about God passes away, you shouldn’t lose your faith. Many rulers and regular people have been tricked into doing wrong things. Only real faith helps a person keep living right until the end.

Zechariah, the son of Jehoiada, was full of God’s Spirit. He stood up and told the people about their sins. That’s what preachers should do—use God’s Word to show people where they’ve gone wrong. But the people stoned Zechariah to death in the courtyard of the temple.

Listen to Zechariah’s last words: “May God see this and punish them!” He wasn’t angry; he was speaking prophetically. Later, God made King Joash very sick. Before the Syrian army left, God struck him down with serious illnesses. When bad things happen, one problem often leads to more problems.

In the end, some of Joash’s own servants killed him. These punishments were hard on him, heavier than anyone could carry. Let’s learn from this and stay honest and faithful to God always.

---

25.o: Amaziah was no enemy to religion, but cool and indifferent friend. Many do what is good, but not with a perfect heart. Rashness makes work for repentance. But Amaziah's obedience to the command of God was to his honour. A firm belief of God's all-sufficiency to bear us out in our duty, and to make up all the loss and damage we sustain in his service, will make his yoke very easy, and his burden very light. When we are called to part with any thing for God and our religion, it should satisfy us, that God is able to give us much more than this. Convinced sinners, who have not true faith, always object to self-denying obedience. They are like Amaziah; they say, But what shall we do for the hundred talents? What shall we do if by keeping the sabbath holy we lose so many good customers? What shall we do without this gain? What shall we do if we lose the friendship of the world? Many endeavour to quiet their consciences by the pretence that forbidden practices are necessary. The answer is, as here, The Lord is able to give thee much more than this. He makes up, even in this world, for all that is given up for his sake.
25.m:
Amaziah wasn't against religion, but he wasn't a big supporter either. Some people do good things, but not because they really mean it. Being hasty often leads to regret later on. However, Amaziah obeyed God’s command, which showed him in a good light. Truly believing that God will take care of you when you do what’s right makes following God easier. It helps to know that God can provide more than what you might lose by doing the right thing. 

When someone asks you to give up something for God or your beliefs, remember that God can give you far more than what you’re giving up. People who realize they’ve done wrong sometimes try to avoid obeying fully. Like Amaziah, they ask, “What will we do about those one hundred talents?” Or, “What if we lose money by keeping the Sabbath day holy?” They worry about losing business or income. Others wonder what they’ll do without certain benefits.

Some pretend that sinful actions are necessary. But the truth is, God can give you much more than what you might lose. In this life alone, God provides for those who give up something for His sake.

25.o: To worship the gods of those whom Amaziah had conquered, who could not help their own worshippers, was the greatest absurdity. If men would consider how unable all those things are to help them, to which they look whenever they forsake God, they would not be such enemies to themselves. The reproof God sent by a prophet was too just to be answered; but he was bidden not to say a word more. The secure sinner rejoices to have silenced his reprovers and monitors; but what comes of it? Those that are deaf to reproof, are ripening for destruction.
25.m:
It was silly for Amaziah to worship the gods of people he had defeated, since those gods couldn't even help their own followers. People should think about how useless these things are when they leave God. They wouldn’t hurt themselves so much if they did.

God sent a prophet to warn Amaziah, which was fair enough. But then the prophet was told to stop speaking. Sinners often feel happy when they silence those who try to correct them. But what happens next? People who ignore warnings end up facing worse trouble.

25.o: Never was a proud prince more thoroughly mortified than Amaziah by Joash king of Israel. A man's pride will bring him low, Pr 29:23; it goes before his destruction, and deservedly brings it on. He that exalteth himself shall be abased. He that goes forth hastily to strive, will not know what he shall do in the end thereof, when his neighbour has put him to shame, Pr 25:8. And what are we when we offer to establish our own righteousness, or presume to justify ourselves before the Most High God, but despicable thistles, that fancy themselves stately cedars? And are not various temptations, is not every corruption, a wild beast of the desert, which will trample on the wretched boaster, and tread his haughty pretensions to the dust? A man's pride shall bring him low; his ruin may be dated from his turning from the Lord.
25.m:
Never was a proud ruler humbled more completely than Amaziah was by Joash, king of Israel. Pride leads to downfall. It comes before destruction and brings it on. Whoever raises himself up will be brought down. Someone who rushes into conflict without thinking will later regret it when their neighbor shames them. 

When we try to prove we are righteous or think too highly of ourselves before God, aren't we just like weeds pretending to be tall cedar trees? Aren't all our sins and temptations like wild beasts that crush our pride?

Pride causes people to fall. We can trace many ruins back to someone turning away from God.

26.o: As long as Uzziah sought the Lord, and minded religion, God made him to prosper. Those only prosper whom God makes to prosper; for prosperity is his gift. Many have owned, that as long as they sought the Lord, and kept close to their duty, they prospered; but when they forsook God, every thing went cross. God never continues either to bless the indolent or to withhold his blessing from the diligent. He will never suffer any to seek his face in vain. Uzziah's name was famed throughout all the neighbouring countries. A name with God and good people makes truly honourable. He did not delight in war, nor addict himself to sports, but delighted in husbandry.
26.m:
As long as Uzziah focused on seeking the Lord and practicing religion, God helped him succeed. Only those who have God’s favor really prosper, because success comes from him. Many people have said that they succeeded while staying faithful to God, but things got hard once they stopped following him. 

God won’t keep blessing lazy people, but he won’t stop helping those who work hard. Anyone who looks for God will find him. Uzziah became well-known in nearby lands. Having a good reputation with God and others is true honor. Instead of enjoying wars or games, Uzziah loved farming.

26.o: The transgression of the kings before Uzziah was, forsaking the temple of the Lord, and burning incense upon idolatrous altars. But his transgression was, going into the holy place, and attempting to burn incense upon the altar of God. See how hard it is to avoid one extreme, and not run into another. Pride of heart was at the bottom of his sin; a lust that ruins many. Instead of lifting up the name God in gratitude to him who had done so much for him, his heart was lifted up to his hurt. Men's pretending to forbidden knowledge, and seeking things too high for them, are owing to pride of heart. The incense of our prayers must be, by faith, put into the hands of our Lord Jesus, the great High Priest of our profession, else we cannot expect it to be accepted by God, Re 8:3. Though Uzziah strove with the priests, he would not strive with his Maker. But he was punished for his transgression; he continued a leper to his death, shut out from society. The punishment answered the sin as face to face in a glass. Pride was at the bottom of his transgression, and thus God humbled him, and put dishonour upon him. Those that covet forbidden honours, forfeit allowed ones. Adam, by catching at the tree of knowledge which he might not eat of, debarred himself of the tree of life which he might have eaten of. Let all that read say, The Lord is righteous. And when the Lord sees good to throw prosperous and useful men aside, as broken vessels, if he raises up others to fill their places, they may rejoice to renounce all worldly concerns, and employ their remaining days in preparation for death.
26.m:
The kings before Uzziah sinned by leaving the Lord’s temple and burning incense on false idols. But Uzziah’s mistake was entering the holy area and trying to burn incense on God’s altar. It shows how difficult it is to avoid one wrong thing without falling into another.

Pride was behind his sin. He should have been thankful to God for all He had done, but instead, his pride led him to do what he shouldn’t. People often try to know things they aren't supposed to because of pride.

Our prayers need to be offered through Jesus, our High Priest, if we want them to be accepted by God, just like in Revelation 8:3. When Uzziah fought against the priests, he didn’t fight against God. For his sin, he became a leper until he died, unable to be around people. His punishment matched his sin, showing how pride caused both.

Those who seek unwanted honors lose the ones they’re allowed to have. Like Adam, who tried to get knowledge he wasn’t meant to have and lost access to the tree of life. We should say, “The Lord is right.”

When God removes successful and helpful people, even if it seems like discarding broken tools, we should trust Him. If He replaces them with others, we can focus on preparing for death rather than worrying about earthly matters.

29.o: When Hezekiah came to the crown, he applied at once to work reform. Those who begin with God, begin at the right end of their work, and it will prosper accordingly. Those that turn their backs upon God's ordinances, may truly be said to forsake God himself. There are still such neglects, if the word be not duly read and opened, for that was signified by the lighting the lamps, and also if prayers and praise be not offered up, for that was signified by the burning incense. Neglect of God's worship was the cause of the calamities they had lain under. The Lord alone can prepare the heart of man for vital godliness: when much good is done in a little time, the glory must be ascribed to him; and all who love him or the souls of men, will rejoice therein. Let those that do good work, learn to do it well.
29.m:
When Hezekiah became king, he started working on reforms right away. People who start by seeking God are doing things the right way, and their efforts will succeed. When people ignore God’s commands, they are really turning away from God himself. 

There are still times today when people don’t pay proper attention to God’s Word. This happens if the scriptures aren’t read regularly, which was shown by keeping the lamps lit. It also happens if prayers and praises aren’t offered, which was shown by burning the incense. 

Neglecting God’s worship led to many troubles. Only God can prepare someone’s heart for true devotion. When good things happen quickly, we should give God the credit. And everyone who loves God or cares about others will be happy about it.

Those who do good should aim to do it well.

29.o: As soon as Hezekiah heard that the temple was ready, he lost no time. Atonement must be made for the sins of the last reign. It was not enough to lament and forsake those sins; they brought a sin-offering. Our repentance and reformation will not obtain pardon but in and through Christ, who was made sin, that is, a sin-offering for us. While the offerings were on the altar, the Levites sang. Sorrow for sin must not prevent us from praising God. The king and the congregation gave their consent to all that was done. It is not enough for us to be where God is worshipped, if we do not ourselves worship with the heart. And we should offer up our spiritual sacrifices of praise and thanksgiving, and devote ourselves and all we have, as sacrifices, acceptable to the Father only through the Redeemer.
29.m:
When Hezekiah learned that the temple was ready, he acted quickly. They needed to make things right after the wrongs of the past king. Just feeling sorry wasn’t enough—they had to offer sacrifices for their sins. 

Our turning away from sin won’t get us forgiveness unless we trust in Jesus, who became a sacrifice for us. As the offerings were placed on the altar, the Levites sang praises. Even when we’re sad about our mistakes, we shouldn’t stop praising God.

The king and everyone agreed with what was happening. It’s not enough just to be around people worshipping God—we need to join in with our hearts too. We should give thanks and sing praises to God, offering them as gifts that come from our devotion, which is only accepted because of Jesus.

30.o: Hezekiah made Israel as welcome to the passover, as any of his own subjects. Let us yield ourselves unto the Lord. Say not, you will do what you please, but resolve to do what he pleases. We perceive in the carnal mind a stiffness, an obstinacy, an unaptness to compel with God; we have it from our fathers: this must be overcome. Those who, through grace, have turned to God themselves, should do all they can to bring others to him. Numbers will be scorners, but some will be humbled and benefited; perhaps where least expected. The rich mercy of God is the great argument by which to enforce repentance; the vilest who submit and yield themselves to the Lord, seek his grace, and give themselves to his service, shall certainly be saved. Oh that messengers were sent forth to carry these glad tidings to every city and every village, through every land!
30.m:
Hezekiah welcomed people from Israel to celebrate Passover just as warmly as he would his own people. 

Let’s give ourselves to the Lord. Don’t say you’ll do whatever you want. Instead, decide to do what He wants.

We see stubbornness and unwillingness in human nature, passed down from our ancestors. This attitude needs to change.

Those who turn to God because of His grace should help others do the same.

Some may mock, but others might humble themselves and benefit.

God’s great mercy is the best reason to encourage people to repent.

Even the worst sinners can find salvation if they ask for God’s grace and serve Him.

It would be wonderful if messengers could spread this good news everywhere—to every city and village across the land.

30.o: The great thing needful in attendance upon God in solemn ordinances, is, that we make heart-work of it; all is nothing without this. Where this sincerity and fixedness of heart are, there may yet be many things short of the purification of the sanctuary. These defects need pardoning, healing grace; for omissions in duty are sins, as well as omissions of duty. If God should deal with us in strict justice, even as to the very best of our doings, we should be undone. The way to obtain pardon, is to seek it of God by prayer; it must be gotten by petition through the blood of Christ. Yet every defect is sin, and needs forgiveness; and should be matter to humble, but not to discourage us, though nothing can make up for the want of a heart prepared to seek the Lord.
30.m:
The most important thing when attending worship services is to have a sincere heart. Without this, everything else doesn’t really count. Even if you’re sincere, you might still miss some parts of what’s expected. These mistakes need God’s forgiving love. Not doing what you should is just as bad as not doing anything at all. 

If God dealt with us based on strict rules, even our best actions wouldn’t save us. To get forgiveness, we must ask God in prayer. We need to ask for mercy through Jesus’ sacrifice. But remember, each mistake is a sin and needs forgiveness. This shouldn’t discourage you, but it should humble you. Having a sincere heart is what truly matters when seeking God.

30.o: Many prayers were put up to God with the peace-offerings. In these Israel looked to God as the God of their fathers, a God in covenant with them. There was also abundance of good preaching. The Levites read and explained the Scriptures. Faith cometh by hearing, and true religion preaching has abounded. They sang psalms every day: praising God should be much of our work in religious assemblies. Having kept the seven days of the feast in this religious manner, they had so much comfort in it, that they kept other seven days also. This they did with gladness. Holy duties should be done with holy gladness. And when sinners humble themselves before the Lord, they may expect gladness in his ordinances. Those who taste this happiness will not soon grow weary of it, but will be glad to prolong their enjoyment.
30.m:
People prayed often during the peace offerings. They asked God, the God of their ancestors and the God who made promises to them, for help.

There was plenty of good preaching too. The Levites read from the Scriptures and explained what they meant. People learn faith by listening, and true religion comes through preaching.

They sang songs to praise God each day. Praising God should be an important part of worship services.

After spending seven days at the feast in this way, they found it so enjoyable that they added another seven days. They did all this with joy. 

When people do their religious duties sincerely, they can find joy in doing them. Sinners who come humbly before God can look forward to finding happiness in His teachings. Once someone experiences this kind of joy, they usually want more of it and don't get tired of it quickly.

32.o: Those who trust God with their safety, must use proper means, else they tempt him. God will provide, but so must we also. Hezekiah gathered his people together, and spake comfortably to them. A believing confidence in God, will raise us above the prevailing fear of man. Let the good subjects and soldiers of Jesus Christ, rest upon his word, and boldly say, Since God is for us, who can be against us? By the favour of God, enemies are lost, and friends gained.
32.m:
People who trust God should still take normal steps to stay safe. God helps those who help themselves. 

Hezekiah brought everyone together and gave them encouraging words. When we truly believe God will protect us, we won’t be afraid of other people.

Good followers of Jesus can rely on what he says. They can feel brave because if God is on our side, who could ever defeat us?

With God’s help, our enemies become weak, and new friends join us.

32.o: God left Hezekiah to himself, that, by this trial and his weakness in it, what was in his heart might be known; that he was not so perfect in grace as he thought he was. It is good for us to know ourselves, and our own weakness and sinfulness, that we may not be conceited, or self-confident, but may always live in dependence upon Divine grace. We know not the corruption of our own hearts, nor what we shall do if God leaves us to ourselves. His sin was, that his heart was lifted up. What need have great men, and good men, and useful men, to study their own infirmities and follies, and their obligations to free grace, that they may never think highly of themselves; but beg earnestly of God, that he will always keep them humble! Hezekiah made a bad return to God for his favours, by making even those favours the food and fuel of his pride. Let us shun the occasions of sin: let us avoid the company, the amusements, the books, yea, the very sights that may administer to sin. Let us commit ourselves continually to God's care and protection; and beg of him never to leave us nor forsake us. Blessed be God, death will soon end the believer's conflict; then pride and every sin will be abolished. He will no more be tempted to withhold the praise which belongs to the God of his salvation.
32.m:
God allowed Hezekiah to face a test to show that he wasn't as strong in faith as he thought. This helped Hezekiah see his weaknesses and not become too proud. Knowing our flaws helps us stay humble and rely on God’s help all the time.

We don’t always realize how weak we are until God lets us face challenges alone. Hezekiah became proud because of his success. Important people should remember their faults and ask God to keep them humble.

Hezekiah didn’t use God’s blessings well. Instead, he let his pride grow from those gifts. We should avoid things that might lead us to sin, such as certain friends, activities, or even places that could tempt us.

We should ask God to protect us and never leave us alone. Death will one day end all struggles for believers. Then there won’t be any more temptation to take credit for God’s work.

33.o: We have seen Manasseh's wickedness; here we have his repentance, and a memorable instance it is of the riches of God's pardoning mercy, and the power of his renewing grace. Deprived of his liberty, separated from his evil counsellors and companions, without any prospect but of ending his days in a wretched prison, Manasseh thought upon what had passed; he began to cry for mercy and deliverance. He confessed his sins, condemned himself, was humbled before God, loathing himself as a monster of impiety and wickedness. Yet he hoped to be pardoned through the abundant mercy of the Lord. Then Manasseh knew that Jehovah was God, able to deliver. He knew him as a God of salvation; he learned to fear, trust in, love, and obey him. From this time he bore a new character, and walked in newness of life. Who can tell what tortures of conscience, what pangs of grief, what fears of wrath, what agonizing remorse he endured, when he looked back on his many years of apostacy and rebellion against God; on his having led thousands into sin and perdition; and on his blood-guiltiness in the persecution of a number of God's children? And who can complain that the way of heaven is blocked up, when he sees such a sinner enter? Say the worst against thyself, here is one as bad who finds the way to repentance. Deny not to thyself that which God hath not denied to thee; it is not thy sin, but thy impenitence, that bars heaven against thee. (2Ch 33:21-25)
33.m:
We saw Manasseh do wicked things, but now he repents. This shows us how much God forgives those who turn away from their sins. When Manasseh lost his freedom and was taken away from his friends who were bad, he started thinking about what he did wrong. He asked God for forgiveness and felt sorry for all the sinful things he did. 

Manasseh believed God would forgive him because God is very merciful. He realized that only God could save him. So, Manasseh began to respect, trust, love, and follow God. After this, Manasseh changed his ways and lived differently.

Imagine the pain and guilt Manasseh felt remembering all the times he disobeyed God, led others astray, and hurt God’s people. But even someone so far gone found a way to repent. We shouldn’t think the path to heaven is closed when we see someone like Manasseh find forgiveness. 

If you’re hard on yourself, remember there was once a person just as bad who found a way to repent. Don’t deny yourself what God offers everyone else—your sins won't keep you out if you truly repent. (2Ch 33:21-25)

33.o: Amon's father did ill, but he did worse. Whatever warnings or convictions he had, he never humbled himself. He was soon cut off in his sins, and made a warning for all men not to abuse the example of God's patience and mercy to Manasseh, as an encouragement to continue in sin. May God help us to be honest to ourselves, and to think aright respecting our own character, before death fixes us in an unchangeable state.
33.m: Amon's dad did bad things, but Amon did even worse. Even though he knew better, he never changed his ways. He died while still doing wrong, serving as a warning to everyone not to misuse God’s patience and mercy toward Manasseh as an excuse to keep sinning. Let’s hope God helps us be honest about ourselves and think correctly about who we are before it’s too late.

35.o: The destruction Josiah made of idolatry, was more largely related in the book of Kings. His solemnizing the passover is related here. The Lord's supper resembles the passover more than any other of the Jewish festivals; and the due observance of that ordinance, is a proof of growing piety and devotion. God alone can truly make our hearts holy, and prepare them for his holy services; but there are duties belonging to us, in doing which we obtain this blessing from the Lord.
35.m:
Josiah got rid of all the false gods, which is described more fully in the book of Kings. Here, the story talks about him celebrating the Passover festival. 

The Lord’s Supper is similar to the Passover, more so than any other Jewish holiday. Properly following this tradition shows that someone is becoming more devoted and pious.

Only God can really make our hearts pure and ready for his worship. But there are things we need to do to get that help from God.

35.o: The Scripture does not condemn Josiah's conduct in opposing Pharaoh. Yet Josiah seems to deserve blame for not inquiring of the Lord after he was warned; his death might be a rebuke for his rashness, but it was a judgment on a hypocritical and wicked people. He that lives a life of repentance, faith, and obedience, cannot be affected by the sudden manner in which he is removed. The people lamented him. Many mourn over sufferings, who will not forsake the sins that caused God to send them. Yet this alone can turn away judgments. If we blame Josiah's conduct, we should be watchful, lest we be cut down in a way dishonourable to our profession.
35.m:
The Bible doesn't say Josiah did wrong by standing up to Pharaoh. But Josiah may have made a mistake by not asking God what to do after being warned. His death could have been because of his carelessness, but it was really a punishment for the people’s hypocrisy and sinfulness.

Someone who lives a good life of turning from sin, believing in God, and obeying Him won’t be hurt by dying suddenly. People were sad about Josiah’s death. Some feel sorry about their troubles without giving up the sins that brought those troubles. Only stopping those sins can stop more trouble.

If we think Josiah acted wrongly, we need to be careful so we don’t face an embarrassing end too.

36.o: The ruin of Judah and Jerusalem came on by degrees. The methods God takes to call back sinners by his word, by ministers, by conscience, by providences, are all instances of his compassion toward them, and his unwillingness that any should perish. See here what woful havoc sin makes, and, as we value the comfort and continuance of our earthly blessings, let us keep that worm from the root of them. They had many times ploughed and sowed their land in the seventh year, when it should have rested, and now it lay unploughed and unsown for ten times seven years. God will be no loser in his glory at last, by the disobedience of men. If they refused to let the land rest, God would make it rest. What place, O God, shall thy justice spare, if Jerusalem has perished? If that delight of thine were cut off for wickedness, let us not be high-minded, but fear.
36.m:
The destruction of Judah and Jerusalem happened slowly over time. When God tries to bring people back to him through his word, through preachers, through their conscience, or through events in life, he shows his care and doesn't want anyone to be lost. 

We see here how much damage sin causes. To protect our blessings, we need to stop sinning. Many times, they worked their fields during the Sabbath year when it was supposed to rest. Now, their land lies empty and uncared for for ten times seven years.

God won’t lose anything in the end because of human disobedience. Since they wouldn’t let the land rest, God made sure it did. If even Jerusalem, which God loved so much, was destroyed for its sins, then where else might God’s justice strike? Let’s not think too highly of ourselves but instead be afraid.

36.o: God had promised the restoring of the captives, and the rebuilding of Jerusalem, at the end of seventy years; and that time to favour Zion, that set time, came at last. Though God's church be cast down, it is not cast off; though his people be corrected, they are not abandoned; though thrown into the furnace, they are not lost there, nor left there any longer than till the dross be separated. Though God contend long, he will not contend always. Before we close the books of the Chronicles, which contain a faithful register of events, think what desolation sin introduced into the world, nay, even into the church of God. Let us tremble at what is here recorded, while in the character of some few gracious souls, we discover that the Lord left not himself without witness. And when we have looked at this faithful portrait of man by nature, let us contrast with it that same nature, when recovered by Almighty grace, through the justifying and soul-adorning righteousness of Christ our Saviour.
36.m:
God promised to free the captives and rebuild Jerusalem after seventy years. The time finally came for God to help Zion. Even if God’s church seems broken, it isn’t forgotten. People might be punished, but they aren't abandoned. They may face trials, but God won’t leave them there forever. He separates the good from the bad.

Though God may punish for a long time, he doesn’t do it forever. As we finish reading the books of Chronicles, remember how sin brought destruction to the world and even to God’s church. We should feel scared about what we see here. But we also see that God didn’t give up on anyone.

In these records, we find some good people who followed God. When we look at the picture of humanity before God’s grace, compare it to how people are changed by God’s power through Jesus. His righteousness saves and beautifies souls.
